title: Deterministic positioning of circular Bragg gratings using atomic force lithography for high-performance quantum dot light sources
authors: Sai Abhishikth Dhurjati, Moritz Langer, Yared G. Zena, Ahmad Rahimi, Liesa Raith, Martin Bauer, Frank H. P. Fitzek, Riccardo Bassoli, Caspar Hopfmann
abstract: Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) grown by molecular beam epitaxy are excellent quantum emitters, but their random spatial distribution hinders deterministic coupling to optical microcavities. We demonstrate a room-temperature atomic force microscopy (AFM)-assisted nano-oxidation lithography technique enabling QD positioning with a radial displacement of $51(28)$ nm. Free-standing asymmetric circular Bragg gratings incorporating AFM-positioned GaAs QDs exhibit a $245$-fold photoluminescence enhancement and fine-structure splitting (FSS) comparable to bulk QDs. Polarization-resolved spectroscopy and finite-difference time-domain simulations show robust emission for displacements up to $50$ nm (Stokes parameter $\lvert S \rvert < 0.05$ ). The devices display stable FSS and polarization imbalance below $5 \, \%$ , confirming precise, reproducible alignment and potential for high fidelity devices. This scalable approach enables deterministic integration of high-performance QDs with photonic cavities, advancing practical quantum light sources for quantum information technologies.
summary: The researchers developed a method using AFM-assisted lithography to precisely position quantum dots within optical microcavities. This technique achieves significant light enhancement and stable polarization, providing a scalable approach for creating high-performance quantum light sources.
main_problem: The random spatial distribution of semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) grown via molecular beam epitaxy prevents deterministic coupling to optical microcavities, which is essential for scalable quantum light sources.
main_result: The authors demonstrated an AFM-assisted nano-oxidation lithography technique that enables deterministic QD positioning with a radial displacement of 51(28) nm, achieving a 245-fold photoluminescence enhancement in circular Bragg gratings.
method: Room-temperature atomic force microscopy (AFM)-assisted nano-oxidation lithography is used to position GaAs QDs within asymmetric circular Bragg gratings.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of GaAs semiconductor quantum dots embedded within free-standing asymmetric circular Bragg gratings.
key_observables: Photoluminescence enhancement, fine-structure splitting (FSS), polarization-resolved spectroscopy, Stokes parameters, and polarization imbalance.
important_parameters: Radial displacement of 51(28) nm, 245-fold photoluminescence enhancement, and polarization imbalance below 5%.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of random QD distribution, describes the AFM-assisted lithography technique, presents the experimental results for QD positioning and photoluminescence enhancement, and concludes with the implications for quantum information technologies.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and photonic quantum information as it provides a scalable, experimental pathway to creating high-fidelity, deterministic single-photon sources by precisely controlling the light-matter interface.
